store.name: Music
store.description: Bands, members, albums, and singles — centred on New Order and connected artists.

field_values.name: New Order
field_values.description: Formed in 1980 from Joy Division after the death of Ian Curtis. Pioneered the fusion of post-punk guitar with electronic dance music and became one of the best-selling bands of the 1980s.

field_values.name: Joy Division
field_values.description: Seminal Manchester post-punk band active 1976–1980. Disbanded following the death of vocalist Ian Curtis on the eve of their first North American tour.

field_values.name: Electronic
field_values.description: Side project of Bernard Sumner (New Order) and Johnny Marr (The Smiths), with occasional contributions from Neil Tennant of Pet Shop Boys. Released three studio albums.

field_values.name: The Smiths
field_values.description: Iconic Manchester indie band formed by Morrissey and Johnny Marr. Widely regarded as one of the most influential bands of the 1980s.

field_values.name: The Other Two
field_values.description: Side project of New Order members Stephen Morris and Gillian Gilbert. Released two studio albums between 1993 and 1999.

field_values.name: Monaco
field_values.description: Band formed by Peter Hook and New Order crew member David Potts while New Order was on hiatus during the mid-1990s.

field_values.name: Bad Lieutenant
field_values.description: Band formed by Bernard Sumner and Phil Cunningham during New Order hiatus following the departure of Peter Hook. Released one album, Never Cry Another Tear (2009).

field_values.name: Revenge
field_values.description: Band formed by Peter Hook during New Order's first extended hiatus. Released one studio album, One True Passion (1990).

field_values.name: Peter Hook & The Light
field_values.description: Band formed by Peter Hook after his departure from New Order, primarily performing Joy Division and New Order material in full album live sets.

field_values.name: Pet Shop Boys
field_values.description: Duo of Neil Tennant and Chris Lowe. One of the best-selling music acts of all time. Neil Tennant contributed vocals to several Electronic recordings.

field_values.name: Bernard Sumner
field_values.description: Co-founder of Joy Division and New Order. Also formed Electronic with Johnny Marr and Bad Lieutenant with Phil Cunningham. Born Bernard Dicken in Salford.

field_values.name: Peter Hook
field_values.description: Co-founder of Joy Division and New Order, known for melodic bass lines played high on the neck. Left New Order in 2007 and subsequently formed Peter Hook and The Light.

field_values.name: Stephen Morris
field_values.description: Drummer and percussionist for Joy Division and New Order. Formed The Other Two with his partner Gillian Gilbert.

field_values.name: Gillian Gilbert
field_values.description: Keyboardist of New Order, joining shortly after the band formed in 1980. Partner of Stephen Morris; together they form The Other Two.

field_values.name: Ian Curtis
field_values.description: Vocalist and lyricist of Joy Division. His death by suicide on 18 May 1980 led the three surviving members to reform as New Order.

field_values.name: Johnny Marr
field_values.description: Guitarist and co-founder of The Smiths. Founded Electronic with Bernard Sumner. Has subsequently had an extensive solo career and collaborated widely.

field_values.name: Phil Cunningham
field_values.description: Joined New Order in 2001, initially as a replacement for Gillian Gilbert. Also a founding member of Bad Lieutenant alongside Bernard Sumner.

field_values.name: David Potts
field_values.description: New Order crew member and guitarist who co-founded Monaco with Peter Hook during New Order hiatus in the mid-1990s.

field_values.name: Morrissey
field_values.description: Vocalist and lyricist of The Smiths, co-founding the band with Johnny Marr in 1982. Born Steven Patrick Morrissey in Davyhulme, Manchester. Pursued a solo career after The Smiths disbanded in 1987.

field_values.name: Andy Rourke
field_values.description: Bassist of The Smiths throughout their entire career. Died in May 2023. Known for fluid, melodic bass playing that complemented Johnny Marr's guitar work.

field_values.name: Mike Joyce
field_values.description: Drummer of The Smiths throughout their career. Later sued Morrissey and Marr over royalties in a high-profile court case, which he won in 1996.

field_values.name: Neil Tennant
field_values.description: Co-founder and vocalist of Pet Shop Boys. Contributed vocals to several Electronic recordings, including tracks on their self-titled debut album.

field_values.title: Unknown Pleasures
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Debut studio album by Joy Division. Produced by Martin Hannett, its sparse, atmospheric sound defined the post-punk genre.

field_values.title: Closer
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Second and final studio album by Joy Division, released two months after Ian Curtis's death. Often considered one of the greatest albums ever made.

field_values.title: Movement
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Debut album by New Order, recorded before the band had fully settled on their electronic direction. Retains a strong Joy Division influence.

field_values.title: Power, Corruption & Lies
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Second studio album. The standalone single Blue Monday, released the same year, became the best-selling 12-inch single of all time.

field_values.title: Low-life
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Third studio album, widely regarded as the point where New Order fully synthesised post-punk guitar with electronic dance music.

field_values.title: Brotherhood
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Fourth studio album, split roughly between guitar-led songs on side one and electronic tracks on side two.

field_values.title: Technique
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Fifth studio album, recorded partly in Ibiza during the acid house explosion. Debuted at number one in the UK.

field_values.title: Republic
field_values.label: London Records
field_values.description: Sixth studio album, released on London Records after Factory Records went bankrupt. Debuted at number one in the UK.

field_values.title: Get Ready
field_values.label: London Records
field_values.description: Seventh studio album, featuring guest appearances from Bobby Gillespie and Billy Corgan. A more guitar-driven return.

field_values.title: Waiting for the Sirens' Call
field_values.label: Warner Bros.
field_values.description: Eighth studio album, the last to feature Peter Hook. Included the single Krafty featuring Scissor Sisters' Ana Matronic.

field_values.title: Music Complete
field_values.label: Mute Records
field_values.description: Ninth studio album and first without Peter Hook. Produced by various collaborators including Stuart Price, widely praised as a late-career high point.

field_values.title: Electronic
field_values.label: Parlophone
field_values.description: Self-titled debut album by Electronic, featuring contributions from Neil Tennant (Pet Shop Boys) on several tracks.

field_values.title: Raise the Pressure
field_values.label: Parlophone
field_values.description: Second studio album by Electronic. More straightforward in production than the debut, co-produced by Karl Bartos of Kraftwerk.

field_values.title: Twisted Tenderness
field_values.label: Parlophone
field_values.description: Third and final album by Electronic. The band dissolved shortly after its release.

field_values.title: The Smiths
field_values.label: Rough Trade Records
field_values.description: Self-titled debut album by The Smiths. Produced by John Porter and Troy Tate. Launched one of the most distinctive voices in British indie music.

field_values.title: Meat Is Murder
field_values.label: Rough Trade Records
field_values.description: Second studio album by The Smiths. Debuted at number one in the UK and features the landmark title track.

field_values.title: The Queen Is Dead
field_values.label: Rough Trade Records
field_values.description: Third studio album by The Smiths. Consistently ranked among the greatest albums of all time.

field_values.title: Strangeways, Here We Come
field_values.label: Rough Trade Records
field_values.description: Fourth and final studio album by The Smiths, released after the band had already broken up. More polished and orchestral than previous releases.

field_values.title: The Other Two & You
field_values.label: London Records
field_values.description: Debut album by The Other Two (Stephen Morris and Gillian Gilbert), recorded alongside the New Order Republic sessions.

field_values.title: Super Highways
field_values.label: London Records
field_values.description: Second and final album by The Other Two, released in 1999.

field_values.title: Music for Pleasure
field_values.label: Polydor
field_values.description: Debut and only studio album by Monaco, recorded by Peter Hook and David Potts while New Order was on indefinite hiatus.

field_values.title: One True Passion
field_values.label: Factory Records
field_values.description: Debut and only studio album by Revenge, Peter Hook's band formed during New Order's first extended hiatus.

field_values.title: Love Will Tear Us Apart
field_values.description: Joy Division's only UK hit single, released posthumously one month after Ian Curtis's death. Reached number 13 in the UK. One of the most celebrated songs in post-punk history.

field_values.title: Atmosphere
field_values.description: Originally released in France in 1980 as a limited edition, then released as a UK single in 1988 where it reached number 34. A fan favourite and emotional highlight of Joy Division's catalogue.

field_values.title: Ceremony
field_values.description: New Order's debut single, the last song written by Joy Division. Peaked at number 34 in the UK.

field_values.title: Blue Monday
field_values.description: New Order's landmark single, issued as a standalone 12-inch. Peaked at number 9 in the UK and became the best-selling 12-inch single of all time. Never appeared on the original Power, Corruption & Lies LP.

field_values.title: Confusion
field_values.description: Produced by Arthur Baker, reached number 12 in the UK. An early example of New Order's transatlantic dancefloor sound.

field_values.title: Thieves Like Us
field_values.description: New Order single released in 1984, reaching number 18 in the UK.

field_values.title: The Perfect Kiss
field_values.description: Lead single from Low-life, reaching number 46 in the UK.

field_values.title: Sub-culture
field_values.description: Second single from Low-life.

field_values.title: Shellshock
field_values.description: Released as part of the Pretty in Pink soundtrack. Reached number 28 in the UK.

field_values.title: Bizarre Love Triangle
field_values.description: From Brotherhood, remixed by Shep Pettibone and re-released, becoming one of New Order's most-streamed songs. Original release peaked at 56 in the UK.

field_values.title: True Faith
field_values.description: Standalone single released alongside the Substance compilation. Reached number 4 in the UK. One of New Order's definitive songs.

field_values.title: Touched by the Hand of God
field_values.description: New Order single reaching number 20 in the UK.

field_values.title: Fine Time
field_values.description: Lead single from Technique, reaching number 11 in the UK.

field_values.title: Round & Round
field_values.description: Second single from Technique, reaching number 21 in the UK.

field_values.title: World in Motion
field_values.description: New Order's only UK number 1 single, recorded in collaboration with the England national football team for the 1990 World Cup.

field_values.title: Regret
field_values.description: Lead single from Republic, reaching number 4 in the UK. Critically and commercially successful.

field_values.title: Crystal
field_values.description: Lead single from Get Ready, reaching number 8 in the UK.

field_values.title: 60 Miles an Hour
field_values.description: Second single from Get Ready, reaching number 29 in the UK.

field_values.title: Here to Stay
field_values.description: Recorded for the 24 Hour Party People soundtrack, produced by The Chemical Brothers. Reached number 15 in the UK.

field_values.title: Krafty
field_values.description: Lead single from Waiting for the Sirens' Call, reaching number 8 in the UK. Featured guest vocals from Ana Matronic of Scissor Sisters.

field_values.title: Getting Away with It
field_values.description: Electronic's debut single, featuring Neil Tennant on lead vocals. Reached number 12 in the UK.

field_values.title: Get the Message
field_values.description: From the Electronic debut album. Reached number 8 in the UK.

field_values.title: Feel Every Beat
field_values.description: From the Electronic debut album. Reached number 39 in the UK.

field_values.title: Disappointed
field_values.description: Electronic single, reached number 6 in the UK. Featured Neil Tennant on vocals.

field_values.title: Hand in Glove
field_values.description: The Smiths' debut single. Did not chart on the UK Singles Chart but reached number 3 on the UK Indie Chart.

field_values.title: This Charming Man
field_values.description: The Smiths' second single and one of their most celebrated songs. Reached number 25 in the UK on original release; re-issued in 1992, reaching number 8.

field_values.title: What Difference Does It Make?
field_values.description: Third single by The Smiths. Reached number 12 in the UK.

field_values.title: Heaven Knows I'm Miserable Now
field_values.description: The Smiths' first top 10 single, reaching number 10 in the UK. A non-album single.

field_values.title: William, It Was Really Nothing
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single reaching number 17 in the UK.

field_values.title: How Soon Is Now?
field_values.description: Originally a B-side, later released as a single. Reached number 24 in the UK. One of The Smiths' most iconic and enduring songs.

field_values.title: Shakespeare's Sister
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single reaching number 26 in the UK.

field_values.title: The Boy with the Thorn in His Side
field_values.description: From The Queen Is Dead sessions, released as a single in 1985. Reached number 23 in the UK.

field_values.title: Bigmouth Strikes Again
field_values.description: From The Queen Is Dead. Reached number 26 in the UK.

field_values.title: Panic
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single. Reached number 11 in the UK. Famous for the refrain 'Hang the DJ'.

field_values.title: Ask
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single. Reached number 14 in the UK.

field_values.title: Shoplifters of the World Unite
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single. Reached number 12 in the UK.

field_values.title: Sheila Take a Bow
field_values.description: Non-album Smiths single. Reached number 10 in the UK, joint highest chart position for the band during their lifetime.

field_values.title: Girlfriend in a Coma
field_values.description: From Strangeways, Here We Come. Reached number 13 in the UK.

field_values.title: I Started Something I Couldn't Finish
field_values.description: From Strangeways, Here We Come. Reached number 23 in the UK.

field_values.title: Last Night I Dreamt That Somebody Loved Me
field_values.description: From Strangeways, Here We Come. Reached number 30 in the UK. The Smiths' final single.
